messages.text: The first planet he visited belonged to a king who sat on a robe so wide it covered nearly all the ground.

messages.name: King
messages.text: Ah, here is a subject. Approach so I may see you better.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: How can you recognize me as a subject when you have never seen me?

messages.name: King
messages.text: All men are subjects to a king. Authority is simple when one understands command.
The king was not unkind. He believed his authority wise because he only ordered things that would happen anyway.

messages.name: King
messages.text: I command you to yawn if you wish. I command the sun to set when the proper hour arrives.
The little prince found this serious and ridiculous at once. He asked to see a sunset, but the king checked a large calendar.

messages.name: King
messages.text: The sunset will occur at seven forty. My command will be obeyed exactly then.
The next planet held a vain man with a shining hat. He bowed whenever the prince clapped.

messages.name: Vain Man
messages.text: Admire me. I am the finest, richest, and most intelligent man on this planet.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: But you are the only man on this planet.

messages.name: Vain Man
messages.text: That makes admiration easier. Clap again.
The prince left quickly. Pride without friendship felt empty, and praise requested by force sounded like wind in a bottle.
On the next planet a drunkard sat among many empty bottles. His face carried shame before he even spoke.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: Why do you drink?

messages.name: Drunkard
messages.text: To forget that I am ashamed.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: Ashamed of what?

messages.name: Drunkard
messages.text: Ashamed of drinking.
This circle made the prince sad. The next adult was a businessman who claimed to possess the stars because he could calculate their number.

messages.name: Businessman
messages.text: I am serious. I count, write totals, lock the paper, and own five hundred million stars.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: What good is it to possess stars if you never care for them?
The businessman had no answer beyond another calculation. The little prince missed his rose, who was vain, but at least alive.
The king interested him longer than the others because the king at least believed command should fit reality. That made his authority lonely rather than merely foolish.

messages.name: King
messages.text: If I command a general to become a seabird and he fails, the fault is mine. Authority must be reasonable.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: Then command yourself to be less alone.
The king did not answer quickly. On the next planet, the vain man had no such silence; he heard only applause, even before it came.

messages.name: Vain Man
messages.text: Clap, and I shall lift my hat. Admire, and I shall know you are a man of taste.
The drunkard frightened the prince more quietly. Shame had made a prison with no door, and each bottle pretended to be a key.
By the time the businessman began to calculate stars, the prince had learned that adult seriousness could become ridiculous when it forgot care.
Each adult seemed trapped inside one word: command, admire, shame, calculate. The prince carried away those words like samples from empty planets.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: Grown-ups are very strange when one idea becomes their whole sky.
Even the businessman looked poor while claiming stars. He possessed numbers, but he had no sunrise, no flower, and no one to miss him.

messages.name: Businessman
messages.text: I am too busy for sunrise. I have totals to protect.
Those visits did not make him wiser all at once. They made him suspicious of wisdom that never kneels down to water anything.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: A star should belong to someone who can love its light, not only write its number.
So each planet pushed him onward, away from hollow authority and toward the harder authority of care.
